# SWE-bench shows that repository-grounded issue resolution remains largely unsolved

## Core Thesis

SWE-bench defines a hard, execution-verified benchmark for repository-grounded software issue resolution and shows that, under the paper’s reported evaluations, frontier model performance was still extremely weak: the best result was Claude 2 resolving 1.96% of tasks. The retrieval ablations further indicate that the bottleneck is not simply code access in the abstract, but getting the right code context into the model without swamping it.

## Why It Matters for Sapho

This matters because it cuts against loose claims that large language models can already function as reliable software engineers on real codebases. The benchmark is grounded in real GitHub issue and pull-request pairs, requires executable success rather than judged plausibility, and still produces near-zero resolution rates at meaningful repository scale. For Sapho, the paper supports a stricter doctrine: claims about coding agents should be evaluated on repository-grounded, test-verified task completion, and context-selection quality should be treated as a first-class determinant of system performance rather than an implementation detail.

## Key Findings

- SWE-bench evaluates 2,294 issue-resolution tasks drawn from real GitHub issues and pull requests across 12 popular Python repositories, with each task framed as an issue description plus the full repository codebase.
- A generated fix only counts if the patch applies successfully and all associated unit and system tests pass, making success an execution-based criterion rather than a subjective assessment.
- Under the paper’s reported evaluations, the best-performing model, Claude 2, resolved only 1.96% of tasks.
- The benchmark instances are not toy-scale: issue descriptions average 195 words, repositories average 3,010 non-test files and 438K non-test lines, and reference fixes average changes to 1.7 files, 3.0 functions, and 32.8 added-or-removed lines.
- Retrieval quality materially affects outcomes: with BM25 retrieval, Claude 2 dropped from 1.96% resolved at 13k context to 1.22% at 50k context, while oracle retrieval increased resolution to 4.8% and oracle-collapsed retrieval to 5.93%.

## Evidence and Findings

- The benchmark was constructed from roughly 90,000 pull requests and filtered down to 2,294 usable tasks drawn from 12 Python repositories. That supports the conclusion that the benchmark is anchored in real maintenance history, but it also shows deliberate narrowing rather than a raw dump of repository activity, which matters when interpreting what the results do and do not generalize to.
- Each task gives the model an issue description and the repository codebase, then counts success only when the produced patch applies and all associated unit and system tests pass. That supports a much stricter notion of “resolved” than surface-level code generation, which matters because it ties reported performance to executable repository behavior.
- The task instances carry nontrivial testing burden: every instance has at least one fail-to-pass test, 40% have at least two fail-to-pass tests, and the median instance also runs 51 additional tests. That supports the claim that benchmark success requires compatibility with broader repository behavior, not merely patching one obvious failure.
- Repository scale is substantial: average tasks involve codebases with 3,010 non-test files and 438K non-test lines, while gold fixes average 1.7 files, 3.0 functions, and 32.8 changed lines. That combination supports the view that the challenge is not giant patch size but locating and editing the right small region inside a large code environment.
- Reported frontier performance is extremely low: Claude 2, the top model in the paper’s main evaluations, resolved 1.96% of issues. That supports the conclusion that real repository-grounded issue resolution remained largely unsolved under these evaluation conditions, which matters because it rebukes broad automation narratives not backed by test-verified outcomes.
- Retrieval setup changes performance materially: BM25 retrieval worsened as allowed context grew from 13k to 50k tokens, but oracle retrieval improved results from 1.96% to 4.8%, and oracle-collapsed retrieval reached 5.93%. That supports the conclusion that the system is highly sensitive to context localization quality, which matters because better access to the right code appears more valuable than merely supplying more code.

## Contradictions and Tensions

- The most important tension is that more retrieved context did not help under BM25. Performance fell from 1.96% at 13k context to 1.22% at 50k, even though a simple intuition would predict that giving the model more code should improve its odds. The paper therefore cuts against any naive “just add more repository context” story.
- At the same time, oracle retrieval materially improved results to 4.8% and 5.93%, which creates a second tension: access to the right context matters, but current practical retrieval methods were far from delivering it reliably. The bottleneck is not pure model incapacity alone and not retrieval alone; it is the interaction between code localization and generation.
- The benchmark is built from real issue and pull-request history, but it is also heavily filtered: roughly 90,000 pull requests were reduced to 2,294 instances, excluding cases with installation or runtime errors and requiring fail-to-pass test behavior. That means the paper is stronger than synthetic benchmarks but still does not represent the full messiness of production software work.
- Success is defined by patch application and passing tests, which is operationally strong but not identical to full software quality. A fix that is valid in practice but not captured by the benchmark’s test harness will not register as success, so the evaluation is rigorous yet still bounded by test coverage.

## Mechanism or Bounds

The strongest supported operational explanation is that repository-grounded issue resolution depends heavily on context localization: models need the right slice of code evidence, and supplying larger amounts of imperfectly retrieved context can degrade rather than improve performance. The benchmark statistics reinforce this interpretation: reference fixes are relatively small, but they must be found inside large repositories with thousands of files and hundreds of thousands of lines. The evidence therefore supports a bounded mechanism in which failure arises from the difficulty of mapping issue text onto the relevant code region and then producing a patch that survives repository-wide execution checks. This is still bounded evidence, not a full causal account of model internals, and all performance claims are specific to the paper’s benchmark construction and retrieval setups.

## Limits

The paper does not establish why performance is low at the level of internal model reasoning; it shows the failure pattern, not a complete causal decomposition.
The benchmark is real-world-derived but filtered, so it excludes part of the operational burden of live software engineering, including instances that break on installation or runtime during benchmark construction.
The evaluation rule is intentionally strict and test-bound, which strengthens comparability but leaves open whether some practically acceptable fixes would be missed.

The paper does not establish why performance is low at the level of internal model reasoning; it shows the failure pattern, not a complete causal decomposition.
The benchmark is real-world-derived but filtered, so it excludes part of the operational burden of live software engineering, including instances that break on installation or runtime during benchmark construction.
The evaluation rule is intentionally strict and test-bound, which strengthens comparability but leaves open whether some practically acceptable fixes would be missed.
